---
description: Dispatch refactor-planner on a file or subject, review the proposed split, apply it mechanically
model: sonnet
---
> Cursor-side mirror of `/refactor`. Source of truth: `.claude/skills/refactor/SKILL.md`.


# /refactor `<target>`

End-to-end refactor flow: plan first, apply mechanically, verify. Behavior-preserving. Any behavior change gets split into its own commit.

## When to run

- A file exceeds its size limit per rule 07 (the size-guard hook denied a Write with this instruction)
- A file's internal responsibilities have become tangled even under the limit
- A reviewer agent flagged refactor-worthy structure

## Procedure

### 1. Plan

Dispatch `refactor-planner` with the target. Expect a `PROPOSED` verdict with:

- The proposed split (files, LOC estimates, responsibilities)
- Public-API boundary
- Migration steps (ordered, mechanical)
- Call-site updates
- ADR necessity

If `NEEDS_CLARIFICATION` — stop and ask the maintainer for the missing context.

If `NOT_RECOMMENDED` — surface the reason, do not proceed. Maintainer may choose the size-exception ADR path instead.

### 2. Review the plan

Show the proposed split to the maintainer **before moving any code**. Get explicit approval. A refactor applied without plan review is how subtle regressions ship.

### 3. ADR (when required)

If the planner flagged ADR-required:

- Draft the ADR under `.claude/decisions/` using the template
- Wait for `meta-reviewer` APPROVE before applying the split

### 4. Apply the split

Execute the migration steps in order:

1. Create new files with the moved content
2. Leave the old file as a barrel re-export only if strictly required for the transition window — preferred path is to update call sites directly
3. Update every call site listed in the plan
4. Run `pnpm typecheck` after each logical step — expect green throughout
5. Run `pnpm test` at the end — expect identical test results to before the refactor
6. Remove any temporary barrel re-exports

### 5. Verify behavior preservation

- Compare test suite output before vs. after — must match
- No new tests added in this refactor commit (refactor is behavior-preserving; new tests belong to follow-ups)
- No public API renames

### 6. Review the refactor

Dispatch `/review` on the resulting diff:

- `code-reviewer` — APPROVE required
- `integration-reviewer` — SAFE or RISKY only; BREAKING means you changed the public API and the refactor scope-crept

### 7. Commit

Conventional-commits message:

```
refactor(<scope>): split <subject> by responsibility

<one paragraph: what was split, why, preserving behavior>

@req: REQ-<scope>-<id>  (or "infrastructure" if none applies)
```

## Output shape

```markdown
# /refactor result — <target>

## Plan

<from refactor-planner>

## Applied changes

| Action            | Path                        | Status |
| ----------------- | --------------------------- | ------ |
| Created           | <new-file>                  | ✓      |
| Moved             | <symbol> from <old> → <new> | ✓      |
| Updated call site | <file>:<line>               | ✓      |

## Verification

- Typecheck: ✓ PASS
- Lint: ✓ PASS
- Test: ✓ PASS (same pass/fail set as pre-refactor)
- /review verdict: APPROVE / SAFE
- /security-review: N/A unless touched security-sensitive files
- ADR: created at .claude/decisions/<id> (if required)

## Commit-ready message

<the conventional-commits subject + body>
```

## What /refactor does NOT do

- Change behavior (even a "small improvement" — behavior changes go in separate commits)
- Add new tests (unless tests are refactored alongside — still behavior-preserving)
- Rename public exports (that's its own decision, own commit, own ADR)
- Move code across package boundaries (that's an architectural change, dispatch `integration-reviewer` first)

## Failure mode

- Test suite results differ before vs. after → `debug-investigator` dispatched; refactor is buggy
- Typecheck red at any step → stop, revert the last step, re-plan
- Call sites the planner missed → pause, update the plan, continue

## Time / cost

Sonnet-tier planning + file moves. Typically 10–30 minutes for a moderate split including review loop.
